cataloging_subject.label: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The two Middle English verse works selected from Carl Horstmann's 1878 article and encoded together as CME00087: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan. This record does not describe Horstmann's complete article, Anglia volume 1, either complete manuscript, the larger five-witness Susanna tradition, or the University of Michigan encoding as intellectual works.

catalog_summary.title: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous poets
catalog_summary.date: Both poems predate their late-fourteenth-century witnesses; Horstmann's selected edition was published in 1878
catalog_summary.region: England; dialect evidence has been used to associate Celestin with the East Midlands and Susan with Yorkshire or northern England, but exact places of composition are unknown

titles.value: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)

assertion.notes: A project-supplied discovery title for the two-work CME unit; neither medieval poem has this collective title.

titles.value: Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna

scope.description: Horstmann's complete 1878 article, from which CME selects only the two poem texts

notes: The article title is useful edition-level access information but is broader than CME00087, which omits both German introductions and a later supplement.

titles.value: All of a story I will you read

notes: Title spelling varies among Pistill, Pistil, and Pistel; DIMEV's conventional title is retained here.

titles.value: There woned in Babylon a bairn in that burgh rich

assertion.notes: DIMEV normalized incipit; spelling is normalized for discovery.

agents.name: Huchown of the Awle Ryale
notes: Andrew of Wyntoun attributes a work called The Pistil of Suet Susane to Huchown; modern identification of both the person and the poem's authorship remains uncertain.

assertion.notes: The two works are cataloged as the products of distinct anonymous poets; the disputed Huchown attribution applies only to Susan.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous poet of Legend of Pope Celestin
Anonymous poet of The Pistill of Susan

assertion.notes: Wyntoun's c. 1420 chronicle attribution probably refers to this title, but it does not securely identify the historical author.

scope.description: The 1878 edited texts selected into CME00087
Transcription from Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 463 and the Vernon Manuscript respectively
Later comparison edition published in 1991; not upstream of CME00087

date_statements.display: Before the end of the fourteenth century; exact composition date unknown
notes: This is a terminus from the manuscript witness, not acceptance of Horstmann's stronger historical conjecture that the poem was more than a century older than the surviving copy.

date_statements.display: End of the fourteenth century, in Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 463
Later fourteenth century; precise date unknown
notes: Peck describes the poem as a later-fourteenth-century adaptation; this dates the work, not any single manuscript copy.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth century, represented by the Vernon and British Library Additional 22283 witnesses
notes: The statement deliberately does not force a single earliest witness where catalog date ranges overlap.

date_statements.display: Published by Carl Horstmann in Anglia 1 (1878)

scope.description: Publication of the immediate source edition represented by CME00087

notes: This is the selected edition's publication date, not necessarily either poem's first-ever publication.
Both works are Middle English. No precise place of composition is securely established.

assertion.notes: Horstmann's 1878 rhyme-based localization is historical dialect analysis, not direct evidence of where the poem was composed.
Peck reports that the dialect suggests southern Yorkshire while also describing the surviving dialect as mixed. This is not promoted to a certain place of composition.

notes: The broad northern association is consistent across the historical and modern sources consulted; surviving manuscripts contain scribal mixtures and do not independently prove origin.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML root also declares German because Horstmann's headings, notes, and editorial matter are German. German is edition-level paratext, not a language of either medieval work.

form_statements.description: Two stanzaic Middle English narrative poems; German prose introductions and editorial notes in Horstmann are not part of the selected work content.
DIMEV records 147 five-line aaabb stanzas; one CME LG container joins two stanzas and therefore yields 146 XML verse groups rather than 147 literary stanzas.

An alliterative poem in 28 thirteen-line ababababcdddc stanzas, followed in Horstmann by a two-line prayer. CME has 29 LG and 365 L because one short printed line is merged into its preceding L element.

subject_statements.label: Celestin's sin, confession, penance, and salvation
The devil and the seven deadly sins

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English religious narratives. In Legend of Pope Celestin, Celestin bargains his soul to the devil for wisdom, rises to the papacy, repents publicly after preaching against the faith, confronts the personified seven deadly sins, and is saved through penitence and Marian intercession. In The Pistill of Susan, two elders falsely accuse Susanna after she rejects them; Daniel exposes their contradictory testimony, vindicates her, and brings the accusers to judgment.

CME00087 contains the two verse texts printed by Horstmann at pages 67–85 and 93–101. It excludes Horstmann's German introductions at pages 55–67 and 85–93 and excludes the separate supplement A Song to Our Lady at pages 390–391. The 2016 U-M revision history also records removal of three prose fragments, confirming that the encoding's intended content boundary is poem text rather than the complete article.

The XML's 175 LG and 1,100 L elements are encoding metrics, not a complete literary stanza count. Celestin has 147 five-line stanzas in DIMEV but 146 LG because one LG joins two stanzas. Susan's print structure has 28 thirteen-line stanzas plus a two-line prayer, but CME has 365 L because one short printed line is merged into the preceding L element.

extent.description: Two selected poems in two XML DIV1 elements. Direct XML counts are 175 LG and 1,100 L: 146 LG/735 L for Celestin and 29 LG/365 L for Susan. These values describe the encoding; the literary structures are 147 five-line Celestin stanzas and 28 thirteen-line Susan stanzas plus a two-line prayer, with the XML discrepancies explained in the part records.

content_parts.label: Legend of Pope Celestin
content_parts.summary: Celestin sells his soul to the devil in exchange for wisdom, becomes pope, sins against the faith, repents, confronts the seven deadly sins, receives help from Mary and angels, and voluntarily accepts execution as the completion of his penance.
notes: DIMEV records 147 five-line stanzas. CME's 146 LG count results from LG 44 containing ten lines and joining two literary stanzas.

Horstmann prints a note after line 470 stating that one or two stanzas of Envy's speech are missing. This is a textual lacuna in the transmitted poem, not proof of physical leaf loss in the present manuscript.

content_parts.label: The Pistill of Susan
content_parts.summary: Susanna rejects the sexual demands of two elders, who accuse her of adultery and secure her condemnation. Daniel separately questions the elders, exposes their incompatible claims about the tree beneath which the alleged act occurred, and vindicates Susanna.
notes: DIMEV describes 28 thirteen-line ababababcdddc stanzas. Horstmann also prints a final two-line prayer. CME's 365 L count is one below the 366 printed lines because the short line 'So ffre' is encoded inside the preceding L element.

The Vernon Manuscript is the direct witness used by Horstmann. British Library Additional 22283, Cotton Caligula A II, Morgan M.818, and Huntington HM 114 are collateral witnesses and must not be placed on the direct CME lineage path.

related_works.title: Susanna (Daniel 13)
notes: The Pistill of Susan adapts the Susanna-and-the-Elders narrative transmitted as Daniel 13 in the Vulgate tradition. The relation applies only to part:susanna.

related_works.title: A Song to Our Lady
notes: Horstmann printed this separate poem as 'Ein Beitrag zu Celestin' at Anglia 1 (1878), 390–391, because it shares Celestin's five-line stanza form. It is not part of CME00087 and is not a continuation of Legend of Pope Celestin.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00087.xml.
evidence.locator: ETS/EEBO/TEXT; two DIV1 elements; revision history dated 2016-09-14
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact encoded titles, two-poem selection boundary, Middle English texts, German editorial matter, manuscript and print milestones, notes, and the revision history recording removal of prose fragments.

evidence.citation: Element and part-boundary audit of repository CME00087.xml.
evidence.locator: Direct counts of DIV1, L, LG, NOTE, PB, and MILESTONE; inspection of LG 44 and the merged short Susan line
evidence.summary: Counts two DIV1, 1,100 L, 175 LG, 39 NOTE, 28 PB, and 227 MILESTONE elements; isolates 735 L/146 LG for Celestin and 365 L/29 LG for Susan and explains why XML grouping differs from literary stanza and printed-line counts.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna,' Anglia 1 (1878), 55–101, complete volume scan at Internet Archive.
evidence.locator: Celestin introduction pp. 55–67 / PDF pp. 67–79 and text pp. 67–85 / PDF pp. 79–97; Susan introduction pp. 85–93 / PDF pp. 97–105 and text pp. 93–101 / PDF pp. 105–113; separate supplement pp. 390–391
evidence.summary: Immediate print source for CME00087 and primary evidence for its part boundaries, Horstmann's witness statements, editorial typography, historical dialect arguments, plot details, lineation, and excluded article material.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, DIMEV 352/IMEV 195 and DIMEV 5607/IMEV 3553, with linked manuscript and bibliography records.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml records 352 and 5607; linked witnesses and bibliography records
evidence.summary: Current scholarly index evidence for conventional titles, normalized incipits, anonymous cataloging, stanza forms, the unique Celestin witness, all five Susan witnesses, manuscript loci, and edition references.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest evidence for Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 463.
evidence.summary: Binds the old and current shelfmarks, end-fourteenth-century catalog date, exact Celestin locus ff. 118v–124va, the non-overlapping public selected image, and the complete-codex imaging request route.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest catalog and visual evidence for the complete Vernon Manuscript facsimile.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete 710-canvas public object, exact Susan locus on fol. 317r–317v / canvases 520–521, decorated three-column layout, shared final target page, and downloadable whole-codex bundle.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Additional MS 22283.
evidence.summary: Confirms one collateral Susan witness, its exact locus, rubric, material, and broad date; it is not Horstmann's source witness.

evidence.citation: Russell A. Peck, ed., 'The Pistel of Swete Susan,' in Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1991), 73–108.
evidence.locator: Introduction printed pp. 73–81 / PDF pp. 74–82; poem printed pp. 82–93 / PDF pp. 83–94; notes printed pp. 94–108 / PDF pp. 95–109
evidence.summary: Modern comparison edition supporting the later-fourteenth-century description, Daniel 13 adaptation, mixed dialect and southern-Yorkshire hypothesis, alliterative form, Wyntoun's Huchown attribution and its uncertainty, witness context, and thematic cataloging.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00087.
evidence.summary: Binds the direct edition-to-witness path, collateral Susan witnesses, complete-manuscript facsimile mappings, exact downloads and local caches, rights statements, request routes, and unresolved source questions to the descriptive record.

open_questions.question: Can Legend of Pope Celestin be dated or localized more securely than its late-fourteenth-century terminus and Horstmann's historical East Midlands rhyme analysis?
next_steps: Consult current specialist work on DIMEV 352, Laud Misc. 463, dialect, rhyme, and the Celestin legend tradition.
Do not promote the witness's place of production or Horstmann's conjectured age to a place or date of composition without independent evidence.

open_questions.question: Does Wyntoun's attribution of The Pistil of Suet Susane to Huchown identify the author of the surviving Middle English poem represented here?
next_steps: Review current Huchown scholarship and textual comparisons rather than treating the chronicle title match as secure authorship.
Retain anonymous primary cataloging and disputed attributed authorship until the identification is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Will the Bodleian supply a complete color facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 463, including all binding and blank surfaces, rather than only the Celestin folios?
next_steps: Request the complete current physical codex through Bodleian Imaging and identify ff. 118v–124va as the associated work portion.
Record any quotation, license, delivery URL, and exact file inventory in the lineage manifest before reuse.

open_questions.question: Can complete current-volume facsimiles of British Library Additional 22283 and Cotton Caligula A II be obtained for the collateral Susan witnesses?
next_steps: Use the British Library full-reproduction request route documented in the lineage manifest, requesting whole codices and separately naming the Susan folios.
Do not substitute selected public images that do not overlap the work.

notes: The direct source path is part-specific: Celestin derives from Laud Misc. 463 through Horstmann; Susan derives from the Vernon Manuscript through Horstmann. The other four Susan manuscripts are collateral witnesses, not additional upstream sources of CME00087.

Whole-manuscript facsimiles are preferred. The linked lineage record maps each poem's folios or canvases inside the complete cached manuscript object, records incomplete provider coverage where applicable, and distinguishes private research caches from redistributable assets.

Horstmann's italic type often represents expanded or supplied manuscript letters and words rather than semantic emphasis. His Celestin note explains that manuscript hooks or strokes on several letters were rendered as italic e, ampersand as italic and, and Jhu as Jesu; comparison against the manuscript remains necessary before reconstructing abbreviation marks.
